neurons.name: LC4
neurons.known_function: Looming detector: responds to expanding dark objects with size-dependent timing. Encodes angular size and expansion velocity.

neurons.name: LPLC2
neurons.known_function: Looming detector: integrates radial optic flow from lobula plate with lobula input. Provides motion-based looming selectivity.

neurons.name: LPLC1
neurons.known_function: Visual projection neuron, role in looming circuit unclear. May provide additional motion information.

neurons.name: PVLP011
neurons.known_function: GABAergic interneuron receiving input from LPLC2. Likely provides inhibitory gain control within the looming circuit.

neurons.name: PVLP010
neurons.known_function: Mixed NT interneuron in PVLP. Receives LC4 input and connects to GF. May provide inhibitory modulation via GluCl.

neurons.name: DNp04
neurons.known_function: Descending neuron receiving convergent LC4 + LPLC2 input. Parallel escape pathway to GF, may drive wing-based escape.

neurons.name: Giant Fiber (DNp01)
neurons.nt_evidence: literature (cholinergic in MaleCNS; serotonergic tag in Hemibrain disputed)
neurons.known_function: Master command neuron for escape jump. Integrates ~15,000 synaptic inputs; LC4 + LPLC2 are dominant visual inputs (~4,648 synapses combined). Fires a single spike to trigger rapid escape.

neurons.name: PSI
neurons.known_function: Peripherally synapsing interneuron. Electrically coupled to GF. Drives DLM flight muscles for wing depression during escape take-off.

neurons.name: TTMn
neurons.known_function: Tergotrochanteral muscle motor neuron. Electrically coupled to GF. Directly drives the jump muscle for rapid leg extension.

neurons.name: GFC1
neurons.known_function: Giant fiber coupled neuron. Electrically coupled to GF. Modulates escape motor output, may coordinate leg movements.

neurons.name: GFC2
neurons.known_function: Giant fiber coupled neuron. Electrically coupled to GF. Coordinates escape motor program.

hypotheses.description: Convergent looming computation: LC4 and LPLC2 provide parallel, complementary looming signals to the GF. LC4 encodes expanding edges (size-velocity), while LPLC2 computes radial optic flow (motion-based). Their convergence on GF (~4,648 combined synapses) implements an AND-like gate requiring both feature types for reliable looming detection, reducing false positives from non-looming stimuli.

Inhibitory gain control via PVLP interneurons: GABAergic (PVLP011) and glutamatergic/GABAergic (PVLP010) interneurons receive input from LC4/LPLC2 and inhibit the GF. This creates a feedforward inhibition motif that raises the GF's firing threshold, ensuring escape is only triggered by strong, sustained looming signals. This may implement a size-threshold mechanism where escape is triggered only when the approaching object reaches a critical angular size.
supporting_evidence: Hemibrain connectivity (245 + 50 inhibitory synapses onto GF)

hypotheses.description: Parallel escape pathways: LC4 and LPLC2 both project to DNp04, a separate descending neuron, in addition to the GF. This suggests a dual-pathway architecture where the GF mediates fast, stereotyped escape jumps (via electrical synapses to TTMn), while DNp04 may drive slower, more flexible escape behaviors such as directional wing-based takeoff.

Electrical synapses ensure speed and reliability: The GF→TTMn and GF→PSI connections use gap junctions (ShakB/Innexin-dependent), not chemical synapses. This enables sub-millisecond transmission latency, critical for the ~5ms brain-to-muscle escape response. The electrical coupling also makes transmission virtually failure-proof, unlike chemical synapses that can undergo short-term depression.

Lateral excitation between LC4 and LPLC2 may sharpen looming selectivity: The small reciprocal connections between LC4 and LPLC2 (~15-21 synapses) could provide positive feedback during genuine looming stimuli, amplifying the combined signal to GF when both pathways are co-activated by a true approaching object.
supporting_evidence: Hypothetical — functional significance not directly tested
